title: Access Specifiers
description: Protect an object's sensitive data from the outside world.

slides.title: Privacy Matters 🔒
slides.content:
By now, you are quite familiar with the `public` keyword that appears in all of our class examples. 

The `public` keyword is an **access specifier**.

slides.title: Public vs Private 🎭
slides.content:
There are three specifiers:
1. `public` - accessible from outside the class
2. `private` - cannot be accessed (or viewed) from outside the class
3. `protected` - exactly like private, except they can be accessed in inherited child classes.

slides.title: Private Example
slides.content: Attempting to touch a private variable.

quizData.question: Why would anyone want to make a variable private?

options.text: To strictly control how outside code interacts with sensitive data, preventing accidental hacks or invalid states

quizData.explanation: If a User object has an `isAdmin` flag, you want to make it private so outside code can't accidentally toggle it back and forth using dot syntax without going through secure checks.

slides.title: Encapsulation 💊
slides.content:
The concept of hiding "sensitive" data from users by making variables `private` is known as **Encapsulation**.

To allow the outside world to carefully read or modify private data later, you write `public` "get" and "set" methods that contain security logic.

quizData.question: If you don't explicitly write `public:` or `private:` at the top of a class, C++ automatically decides that all members are `private` by default.
quizData.explanation: Yes. Classes are private (closed off) by default. Conversely, Structs are entirely public by default.
